sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hyperkalaemia occurs when extracellular potassium increases, interfering with the normal action potential generation, having a detrimental effect in both skeletal muscle function and profoundly affecting normal cardiac function. Hyperkalaemia is defined as a serum potassium greater than 5.2 mmol (mEq/L) and can occur from any condition that causes an increase in extracellular potassium. Clinical effects depending on severity from mild, moderate to severe. Hyperkalaemia tends to be an incidental finding during examination and normally don’t manifest until there is moderate degree of hyperkalaemia (>6mmol/L).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- General weakness and/or paraesthesia, muscle cramps, paralysis
- Lethargy and confusion
- Nausea, vomiting and/or diarrhoea
- Signs of underlying cause, i.e., renal impairment, burns, metabolic acidosis
- ECG changes consistent with hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- High-risk groups include patients with chronic kidney disease, missed dialysis, crush injury, prolonged downtime, severe acidosis, or potassium-raising medications
- Treat significant ECG change or marked clinical suspicion early and do not wait for laboratory confirmation if the patient is unstable
- The definitive treatment for severe hyperkalaemia is often dialysis, which may influence destination planning
- Calcium gluconate and sodium bicarbonate must not be given together in the same line because of precipitation risk

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Perform ECG monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG where possible
- Perform repeated vital signs
- Consider Secondary or CNS survey
- Escalate early if there is bradycardia, wide QRS, profound weakness, or peri-arrest physiology

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG and assess for changes associated with hyperkalaemia
- Gain IV access and ensure patency
- Fluid resuscitation only if clinically indicated and the patient is not fluid overloaded
- Use point-of-care testing where available if this does not delay treatment of an unstable patient
- Consider salbutamol in consultation with the on-call doctor and local protocol
- Consider calcium gluconate 10% in consultation with the on-call doctor to stabilise the myocardium when significant ECG changes or severe hyperkalaemia are present
- Consider sodium bicarbonate 8.4% in consultation with the on-call doctor when clinically appropriate, particularly if there is associated acidosis
- Consider insulin and glucose in consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Calcium Gluconate provides immediate stabilisation of the myocardium; however, it does not reduce serum potassium levels
- Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% will reduce serum potassium levels by 0.5 – 1 mmol/L and provide temporary effect whilst the underlying cause is treated
- Continuous salbutamol nebuliser reduces serum potassium levels by 0.5 – 1 mmol/L within 30 minutes
- Insulin must always be co-administered with glucose — never administer insulin alone for hyperkalaemia; iatrogenic hypoglycaemia is a serious risk. Monitor blood glucose closely after administration
